nodes.title: Return identity when no functions are provided
nodes.why: `compose()` should still return a callable function. Identity keeps behavior predictable.
nodes.alternative: Throw an error when `fns` is empty.
nodes.tradeoff: Identity is more practical for pipelines, but it can hide accidental empty input.

nodes.title: Call the rightmost function with all incoming args
nodes.why: Only the rightmost function may take multiple arguments, so we start there with `...args`.
nodes.alternative: Force all functions to be unary and accept one value only.
nodes.tradeoff: More flexible API, but callers must understand the rightmost-function rule.

nodes.title: Apply the remaining functions from right to left
nodes.why: Composition order matters. This loop guarantees each function consumes the previous result.
nodes.tradeoff: Loop form is explicit and easy to debug, but slightly longer than reduce-based code.

nodes.title: Keep identity behavior for empty composition
nodes.why: The empty case should match approach 1 so `compose()` stays safe to call.
nodes.tradeoff: Identity keeps APIs stable, though it may mask missing configuration.

nodes.title: Seed reduceRight with rightmost function result
nodes.why: This handles the variadic rightmost function before unary composition continues.
nodes.tradeoff: Clear separation of concerns, but one extra local variable.

nodes.title: Use reduceRight for concise composition
nodes.why: `reduceRight` naturally mirrors right-to-left flow in a compact expression.
nodes.tradeoff: Short and expressive, but some developers find loops easier to step through while debugging.
